name: Regeneration Goblin
oneLiner: Keeps asking one more time, because the next version might finally click.
symptoms: Your chat has become a small saga and the paragraph still isn't quite there
You've caught yourself typing 'almost' and 'one more try' more than once
Part of you believes the next regenerate button has the answer
humanFix: Write down what good looks like before you start prompting
Change one constraint at a time so the model can actually follow you
After three tries, switch from prompting to editing; it is often faster
Save the version that worked so future-you gets a shortcut
verdict: The model is close. Your workflow just needs a finish line.

name: Context Hoarder
oneLiner: Brings the whole attic, just in case one dusty box matters.
symptoms: Your prompts sometimes look like they packed for a long weekend
You've pasted an entire doc 'just for context'
The AI spends half its answer summarising your context before helping
humanFix: Write one sentence describing what the AI truly needs to know
Cut the context to the parts that change the answer
If you cannot say why a detail is included, park it for now
Keep a short reusable setup prompt instead of repasting the whole universe
verdict: Your instinct to be helpful is good. The context just needs a packing list.

name: Confident Answer Collector
oneLiner: Loves a polished answer, then remembers sources exist.
symptoms: You've nearly used an AI stat before checking where it came from
Polished wording sometimes feels reassuring before the sources do
You've had the 'wait, does that actually exist?' moment
humanFix: Treat every factual claim from AI as a polished first draft
Ask for sources, then open the sources before using the claim
For anything external-facing, use AI for structure and do the verification yourself
Build a quick 'does this actually exist?' check into your workflow
verdict: A confident sentence is a great first draft. It still needs a passport check.

name: Prompt Archaeologist
oneLiner: Knows the perfect old prompt is in here somewhere.
symptoms: You have a prompts folder that is more museum than system
You've said 'there was a version of this that worked...' and gone digging
Finding the old prompt can take longer than rewriting the task
humanFix: Keep a single running doc of prompts that have worked, with one-line labels
If a prompt took more than three iterations to get right, save the final version immediately
Review and cull the doc monthly so old experiments do not crowd out useful ones
Name prompts by outcome, not date: client_email_first_reply beats GPT prompt 14 April
verdict: The archive has treasures. It just needs labels.

name: Copy-Paste Pilot
oneLiner: Lets the AI take the first lap, then forgets to grab the wheel.
symptoms: You've sent something that looked fine, then noticed the placeholder later
You have skimmed an AI draft with trust in your heart and a deadline nearby
You could explain the output, but only after rereading it very carefully
Your editing sometimes starts and ends with selecting the useful bit
humanFix: Before you send an AI draft, read it once as if your name is on every word
Change at least one sentence so your judgment enters the room
Ask: could I defend this in a call? If not, give it another pass
Use AI to make a draft, then make the draft yours
verdict: The draft is helpful. The final still wants your fingerprints.

name: The Improviser
oneLiner: Starts every AI task with jazz hands and a blank prompt box.
symptoms: Your approach to AI often begins with 'let's see what happens'
You've solved the same task from scratch more than once
A workflow works beautifully, then disappears into browser history
humanFix: When something works, write down the steps before you close the tab
Pick the three AI tasks you repeat most and make a tiny template for each
Start each session with: I need X for Y, using Z
Treat a good result as a reusable recipe, not a lucky weather event
verdict: Improvisation is useful. A tiny repeatable pattern makes it useful twice.
